excerpts:
M:**
Riad Ali
City Auditor
**SUBJECT:**
Special Request: Affordable Housing Trust Fund (AHTF)
At City Council’s request, the Office of the City Auditor (OCA) reviewed the City’s compliance
with City of Richmond Code Section 12-46 regarding the establishment of potential funding
sources for the Affordable Housing Trust Fund (AHTF).

We recommend that the Chief Administrative Officer (CAO) collaborate with City Council to
take formal action to resolve the identified issues in City Code Sections 12-46. As appropriate,
this may include clarifying legislation, or other actions adopted by City Council and developing
an implementation plan. Following any legislative action, the CAO should:
• Develop formal policies and procedures that define roles and responsibilities, process
steps, timing, definitions, data sources, and monitoring.
•
Ensure required data elements are clearly defined, accurate, and readily available for
calculation and reporting.
•
Establish a reporting process to City Council on the results for timely appropriations and
oversight.

We recommend that the CAO coordinate with City Council to determine the appropriate
disposition of the $2,472,307 credited to the special reserve in FY 2022, including consideration
of appropriation to the AHTF.

We recommend that the CAO collaborate with the Council Chief of Staff to establish a formal
quality review process for proposed ordinances and implementation plans.

We recommend that the CAO work with the Chief of Staff to develop a process that enhances
oversight, accountability, and transparency in the implementation of approved City ordinances.

We recommend that the Director of Finance maintain a separate AHTF within the City’s
accounting structure to improve segregation, tracking, and reporting of AHTF activity and to
reduce the risk of errors or overspending.

We recommend that the Director of Finance establish a documented process to track City
Council approved ordinances that authorize financial transfers and to monitor each transfer
through completion, including reconciliation of ordinance requirements to the City’s financial
system and escalation to the CAO (or designee) when transfers are not executed timely.

The OCA notes that the $2,472,307 of expired partial real estate tax exemptions credited to the
special reserve would still require a City Council appropriation to the AHTF before it could be
used by the AHTF.

With respect to City Code Section 12-46(b), the OCA found that the code does not clearly
specify the frequency or application of the “up to $1,000,000” crediting requirement related to
net proceeds from tax delinquent property sales.

Collectively, these issues resulted in inconsistent reserve crediting and made it difficult to
implement and demonstrate compliance with the City Code requirements as written.

ten.
**1A. The City did not consistently credit the special reserve as required for amounts**
**associated with partial real estate tax exemptions, and credited funds were not**
**appropriated to the AHTF.

excerpts: But the report from City Auditor Riad Ali, done at the request of city council, found money barely made it into the special reserve — and never went to the

The finance director was required to put money from those two streams into a special reserve for the Affordable Housing Trust Fund. But the report from City Auditor Riad Ali, done at the request of city council, found money barely made it into the special reserve — and never went to the trust fund until recent actions by the city.

Ali’s office only found one year — fiscal year 2022 — when Sheila White, the city’s former finance director, credited the tax abatement revenue as required. But the nearly $2.5 million that she put into the reserve wasn’t appropriated to the fund by the council.
The audit concluded that Richmond’s issues with paying revenues into the trust fund partly stemmed from a lack of a documented plan to make sure the city was following its own rules.

Mayor Danny Avula’s administration agreed with the audit’s seven recommendations, which ranged from adopting legislation to clarify city code to establishing formal policies for overseeing funding for the trust fund.

According to the audit, the city hadn’t created a separate dedicated fund for the trust fund as required. The city’s finance department established one during the audit, which the report said should improve the trust fund’s transparency and accounting.

excerpts: On Friday, Feb. 6, Richmond's Office of the City Auditor (OCA) released a report regarding the city's compliance with a law on the books that was meant to

On Friday, Feb. 6, Richmond’s Office of the City Auditor (OCA) released a **[report](https://rva.gov/sites/default/files/2026-02/2026-07%20Special%20Request%20-%20Affordable%20Housing%20Trust%20Fund%20%28AHTF%29.pdf)** regarding the city’s compliance with a law on the books that was meant to provide a funding source for the Affordable Housing Trust Fund (AHTF).
The report contains information that was up-to-date as of late December 2025. Here’s a few of the key findings:

**There was no plan in place to implement the relevant code section** . Implementation of this code section was inconsistent — at least, in part — because the city had no documented plan, nor related procedures, to ensure it was done. **The relevant code section itself is unclear.** The existing code section is vague.

**The Delinquent Tax Sales special fund is at risk of going negative** . City auditors found that $3 million in costs are waiting to be paid out of the special fund — but there’s only about $2.77 million in the fund.

Since the passage of the 2021 ordinance, city code has required that the city’s Director of Finance annually credit funds relating to the expiration of partial real estate tax exemptions to a special reserve for the AHTF.
However, the OCA found that this only ever happened in fiscal year 2022, to the amount of about $2.47 million. It did not take place in any other year.
This $2.47 million was never added to the AHTF and was still in the special reserve at the time of the OCA’s investigation.

The OCA made seven recommendations to the city as to how it can resolve the concerns raised by this report. Five out of the 7 are considered “high priority,” or urgent, while the remaining two are considered “medium priority.”

**Take formal action to resolve the gaps left within existing legislation** regarding funding the AHTF. Develop clear, concise new legislation, as well as formal policies and procedures. **Determine how to appropriately allocate the $2.47 million** that has been sitting in the special reserve relating to expired tax exemptions since 2022. **Establish a formal quality review process** for proposed ordinances and implementation plans. **Develop a process that enhances oversight, accountability and transparency** regarding how ordinances are implemented. Establish a documented process within the Department of Finance to **track approved ordinances relating to financial transfers** and to monitor said transfers through completion. Create a separate AHTF within the city’s accounting structure to **enhance oversight and to reduce the risk of errors or overspending**

*Establish a minimum operating reserve threshold for the Delinquent Tax Sales fund** , implement periodic monitoring of the fund and evaluate if additional funding is needed to ensure approved transfers can be completed without making the fund go negative.

excerpts: On Friday, Feb. 6, Richmond's Office of the City Auditor (OCA) released a report regarding the city's compliance with a law on the books that was meant to

On Friday, Feb. 6, Richmond’s Office of the City Auditor (OCA) released a **[report](https://rva.gov/sites/default/files/2026-02/2026-07%20Special%20Request%20-%20Affordable%20Housing%20Trust%20Fund%20%28AHTF%29.pdf)** regarding the city’s compliance with a law on the books that was meant to provide a funding source for the Affordable Housing Trust Fund (AHTF).
The report contains information that was up-to-date as of late December 2025. Here’s a few of the key findings:

**There was no plan in place to implement the relevant code section** . Implementation of this code section was inconsistent — at least, in part — because the city had no documented plan, nor related procedures, to ensure it was done. **The relevant code section itself is unclear.** The existing code section is vague.

**The Delinquent Tax Sales special fund is at risk of going negative** . City auditors found that $3 million in costs are waiting to be paid out of the special fund — but there’s only about $2.77 million in the fund.

Since the passage of the 2021 ordinance, city code has required that the city’s Director of Finance annually credit funds relating to the expiration of partial real estate tax exemptions to a special reserve for the AHTF.
However, the OCA found that this only ever happened in fiscal year 2022, to the amount of about $2.47 million. It did not take place in any other year.
This $2.47 million was never added to the AHTF and was still in the special reserve at the time of the OCA’s investigation.

The OCA made seven recommendations to the city as to how it can resolve the concerns raised by this report. Five out of the 7 are considered “high priority,” or urgent, while the remaining two are considered “medium priority.”

**Take formal action to resolve the gaps left within existing legislation** regarding funding the AHTF. Develop clear, concise new legislation, as well as formal policies and procedures. **Determine how to appropriately allocate the $2.47 million** that has been sitting in the special reserve relating to expired tax exemptions since 2022. **Establish a formal quality review process** for proposed ordinances and implementation plans. **Develop a process that enhances oversight, accountability and transparency** regarding how ordinances are implemented. Establish a documented process within the Department of Finance to **track approved ordinances relating to financial transfers** and to monitor said transfers through completion. Create a separate AHTF within the city’s accounting structure to **enhance oversight and to reduce the risk of errors or overspending**

*Establish a minimum operating reserve threshold for the Delinquent Tax Sales fund** , implement periodic monitoring of the fund and evaluate if additional funding is needed to ensure approved transfers can be completed without making the fund go negative.

excerpts: But the report from City Auditor Riad Ali, done at the request of city council, found money barely made it into the special reserve — and never went to the

The finance director was required to put money from those two streams into a special reserve for the Affordable Housing Trust Fund. But the report from City Auditor Riad Ali, done at the request of city council, found money barely made it into the special reserve — and never went to the trust fund until recent actions by the city.

Ali’s office only found one year — fiscal year 2022 — when Sheila White, the city’s former finance director, credited the tax abatement revenue as required. But the nearly $2.5 million that she put into the reserve wasn’t appropriated to the fund by the council.
The audit concluded that Richmond’s issues with paying revenues into the trust fund partly stemmed from a lack of a documented plan to make sure the city was following its own rules.

Mayor Danny Avula’s administration agreed with the audit’s seven recommendations, which ranged from adopting legislation to clarify city code to establishing formal policies for overseeing funding for the trust fund.

According to the audit, the city hadn’t created a separate dedicated fund for the trust fund as required. The city’s finance department established one during the audit, which the report said should improve the trust fund’s transparency and accounting.

• Develop formal policies and procedures that define roles and responsibilities, process

Ensure required data elements are clearly defined, accurate, and readily available for
calculation and reporting.
•
Establish a reporting process to City Council on the results for timely appropriations and
oversight.

We recommend that the CAO coordinate with City Council to determine the appropriate
disposition of the $2,472,307 credited to the special reserve in FY 2022, including consideration
of appropriation to the AHTF.

We recommend that the CAO collaborate with the Council Chief of Staff to establish a formal
quality review process for proposed ordinances and implementation plans.

We recommend that the CAO work with the Chief of Staff to develop a process that enhances
oversight, accountability, and transparency in the implementation of approved City ordinances.

We recommend that the Director of Finance maintain a separate AHTF within the City’s
accounting structure to improve segregation, tracking, and reporting of AHTF activity and to
reduce the risk of errors or overspending.

We recommend that the Director of Finance establish a documented process to track City
Council approved ordinances that authorize financial transfers and to monitor each transfer
through completion, including reconciliation of ordinance requirements to the City’s financial
system and escalation to the CAO (or designee) when transfers are not executed timely.

The OCA notes that the $2,472,307 of expired partial real estate tax exemptions credited to the
special reserve would still require a City Council appropriation to the AHTF before it could be
used by the AHTF.

With respect to City Code Section 12-46(b), the OCA found that the code does not clearly
specify the frequency or application of the “up to $1,000,000” crediting requirement related to
net proceeds from tax delinquent property sales.

Collectively, these issues resulted in inconsistent reserve crediting and made it difficult to
implement and demonstrate compliance with the City Code requirements as written.

excerpts:
M:**
Riad Ali
City Auditor
**SUBJECT:**
Special Request: Affordable Housing Trust Fund (AHTF)
At City Council’s request, the Office of the City Auditor (OCA) reviewed the City’s compliance
with City of Richmond Code Section 12-46 regarding the establishment of potential funding
sources for the Affordable Housing Trust Fund (AHTF).

We recommend that the Chief Administrative Officer (CAO) collaborate with City Council to
take formal action to resolve the identified issues in City Code Sections 12-46. As appropriate,
this may include clarifying legislation, or other actions adopted by City Council and developing
an implementation plan. Following any legislative action, the CAO should:
• Develop formal policies and procedures that define roles and responsibilities, process
steps, timing, definitions, data sources, and monitoring.
•
Ensure required data elements are clearly defined, accurate, and readily available for
calculation and reporting.
•
Establish a reporting process to City Council on the results for timely appropriations and
oversight.

We recommend that the CAO coordinate with City Council to determine the appropriate
disposition of the $2,472,307 credited to the special reserve in FY 2022, including consideration
of appropriation to the AHTF.

Collectively, these issues resulted in inconsistent reserve crediting and made it difficult to
implement and demonstrate compliance with the City Code requirements as written.

We recommend that the CAO collaborate with the Council Chief of Staff to establish a formal
quality review process for proposed ordinances and implementation plans.

We recommend that the CAO work with the Chief of Staff to develop a process that enhances
oversight, accountability, and transparency in the implementation of approved City ordinances.

We recommend that the Director of Finance establish a documented process to track City
Council approved ordinances that authorize financial transfers and to monitor each transfer
through completion, including reconciliation of ordinance requirements to the City’s financial
system and escalation to the CAO (or designee) when transfers are not executed timely.

With respect to City Code Section 12-46(b), the OCA found that the code does not clearly
specify the frequency or application of the “up to $1,000,000” crediting requirement related to
net proceeds from tax delinquent property sales.

special reserve would still require a City Council appropriation to the AHTF before it could be
used by the AHTF.

excerpts:
We recommend that the CAO work with the Chief of Staff to develop a process that enhances
oversight, accountability, and transparency in the implementation of approved City ordinances.

We recommend that the CAO collaborate with the Council Chief of Staff to establish a formal
quality review process for proposed ordinances and implementation plans.

We recommend that the Chief Administrative Officer (CAO) collaborate with City Council to
take formal action to resolve the identified issues in City Code Sections 12-46. As appropriate,
this may include clarifying legislation, or other actions adopted by City Council and developing
an implementation plan. Following any legislative action, the CAO should:
• Develop formal policies and procedures that define roles and responsibilities, process
steps, timing, definitions, data sources, and monitoring.
•
Ensure required data elements are clearly defined, accurate, and readily available for
calculation and reporting.
•
Establish a reporting process to City Council on the results for timely appropriations and
oversight.

We recommend that the CAO coordinate with City Council to determine the appropriate
disposition of the $2,472,307 credited to the special reserve in FY 2022, including consideration
of appropriation to the AHTF.

We recommend that the Director of Finance establish a documented process to track City
Council approved ordinances that authorize financial transfers and to monitor each transfer
through completion, including reconciliation of ordinance requirements to the City’s financial
system and escalation to the CAO (or designee) when transfers are not executed timely.

With respect to City Code Section 12-46(b), the OCA found that the code does not clearly
specify the frequency or application of the “up to $1,000,000” crediting requirement related to
net proceeds from tax delinquent property sales.

Collectively, these issues resulted in inconsistent reserve crediting and made it difficult to
implement and demonstrate compliance with the City Code requirements as written.

The OCA notes that the $2,472,307 of expired partial real estate tax exemptions credited to the
special reserve would still require a City Council appropriation to the AHTF before it could be
used by the AHTF.

excerpts: On this page, partners can find resources related to the monitoring and compliance of the City's affordable properties.
citations.title: Asset Management & Compliance Resources

executive_summary.description: A high-level summary of the key findings regarding affordable housing compliance monitoring tools nationally, the specific recommendations from the Richmond City Auditor's report, and the potential models and tools available for the City of Richmond.

city.description: The municipality that uses the compliance tool.

tool_name.description: The official name of the affordable housing compliance tool, portal, or dataset.

description.description: A brief description of the tool's purpose and function.

key_features.description: Notable features of the tool, such as public dashboards, data download capabilities, or online reporting.

data_tracked.description: Examples of the types of data monitored by the tool, such as project status, unit inventory, affordability levels, and tenant income.

national_compliance_tool_profiles.description: Detailed profiles of comparable municipal affordable housing compliance monitoring tools. Each profile will include the city (Denver, San Francisco, Chicago, Los Angeles, DC), the name of the tool/system, its key features, the type of data it tracks, and its primary function in monitoring compliance.

richmond_auditor_report_summary.description: A summary of the findings from the February 2026 Richmond City Auditor report by Riad Ali. This includes details on the failure to implement funding ordinances for the Affordable Housing Trust Fund (AHTF), issues with vague code language, and the financial status of the Delinquent Tax Sales special fund.

recommendation_id.description: The identifier for the recommendation as listed in the auditor's report.

recommendation_summary.description: The specific recommendation made by the City Auditor.

priority.description: The priority level assigned to the recommendation (e.g., High Priority, Medium Priority).

responsible_party.description: The city official or department responsible for implementing the recommendation (e.g., CAO, Director of Finance).

target_completion_date.description: The target date for the implementation of the recommendation.

richmond_auditor_report_recommendations.description: A list of the specific recommendations made by the Richmond City Auditor to address the identified issues with affordable housing compliance and funding infrastructure. This includes recommendations for legislative action, formal quality review processes, enhanced oversight, and improved financial tracking.

system_name.description: The name of Virginia Housing's compliance monitoring system.

key_features.description: Key functionalities of the system, such as XML tenant data uploads and compliance management.

relevance_for_richmond.description: Analysis of whether this state-level system could serve as a model for a local system in Richmond.

key_deadlines.description: Important deadlines associated with the system, such as the March 1 tenant data validation deadline.

nhpd_overview.description: An explanation of the National Housing Preservation Database (NHPD), detailing its purpose, the type of data it collects, and its role in tracking federally-assisted rental housing to support preservation efforts.

property_name.description: The name of the affordable housing property.

address.description: The physical address of the property.

number_of_units.description: The total number of affordable units in the property.

subsidy_program.description: The federal or state subsidy program associated with the property (e.g., LIHTC, Section 8).

nhpd_richmond_properties.description: A list of affordable housing properties located in Richmond, VA, that are tracked within the National Housing Preservation Database (NHPD).

initiative_name.description: The name of the tool or project that originated from the initiative.

location.description: The city or region where the initiative took place.

origin.description: The type of event where the tool was developed (e.g., Hackathon, Civic Tech Sprint).

adoption_status.description: Whether the tool was later formally adopted or used by the city government.

tool_name.description: The name of the open-source tool or framework.

tool_type.description: The category of the tool (e.g., Data Visualization Platform, JavaScript Library, Database).

relevance.description: A brief description of how this tool can be used to build an affordable housing compliance dashboard.

example_of_use.description: A known example of a municipality using this tool for a similar purpose, if available.

opensource_dashboard_frameworks.description: A list of open-source tools, platforms, or frameworks that can be used for building affordable housing compliance dashboards. This includes data visualization libraries, database technologies, and platforms like Tableau Public, which is used by the City of Chicago.

richmond_ahtf_funding_context.description: Detailed context on the issues surrounding Richmond's Affordable Housing Trust Fund (AHTF), including the history of unimplemented ordinances, the commingling of funds, and how these administrative failures created the need for better compliance and monitoring infrastructure.

key_challenges_in_compliance_monitoring.description: A summary of common challenges in affordable housing compliance monitoring, as illustrated by the Richmond audit and national examples. This includes issues like legislative ambiguity, lack of documented procedures, staff turnover, and inadequate financial oversight.

best_practices_for_monitoring_systems.description: A compilation of best practices for developing and maintaining an effective affordable housing compliance monitoring system, based on the features of the national tools and the recommendations from the Richmond audit. Topics include data transparency, clear definitions, regular reporting, and robust financial controls.
